templates.name: Title Hero
templates.whenToUse: Deck openers, hero moments, brand statements where a single emotional payoff carries the slide.
templates.avoidWhen: When you need to introduce multiple ideas at once — use Three Pillars or Bullet List instead.

templates.name: Section Beat
templates.whenToUse: Major chapter breaks in long decks. Gives the audience a 'here's where we are' moment with one giant numeral.
templates.avoidWhen: Decks shorter than 8 slides — every break costs pacing. Use sparingly: 3–5 in a 20-slide deck.

templates.name: Statement Slide
templates.whenToUse: One headline, one paragraph, lots of breathing room. Best for the single most important point in a section.
templates.avoidWhen: When you have 3+ supporting points — use Bullet List or Three Pillars instead.

templates.name: Bullet List
templates.whenToUse: Classic agenda or capability rundown — 3–6 enumerated points. Reach for this when the items are peers, not a process.
templates.avoidWhen: When the items are sequential (use Step Flow) or comparative (use Old Way vs New Way).

templates.name: Three Pillars
templates.whenToUse: Three peer concepts of equal weight — capabilities, customer segments, value props. The rhythm of the deck loves the rule of three here.
templates.avoidWhen: Two ideas (use Before / After) or four+ (drop to Bullet List). Forcing three when only two fit reads as filler.

templates.name: Big Pull Quote
templates.whenToUse: A single quote that earns full-bleed treatment — internal voice, customer testimonial, or industry thinker. Use after a data slide to soften the rhythm.
templates.avoidWhen: Generic platitudes — the quote needs to land. If it doesn't quote-tweet itself, cut it.

templates.name: Triple Stat
templates.whenToUse: Three hero numbers side-by-side at maximum size. The flagship metrics slide of a deck.
templates.avoidWhen: Four+ metrics (use KPI Grid) or qualitative claims dressed as numbers. Each stat must be defensible.

templates.name: KPI Grid
templates.whenToUse: Four metrics in a denser grid with QoQ deltas. Engineering reviews, ops dashboards, board updates.
templates.avoidWhen: Marketing decks where three big numbers tell a stronger story — switch to Triple Stat.

templates.name: Data Table
templates.whenToUse: When the comparison needs rows and right-aligned numerics — pricing tiers, plan comparisons, API limits, score thresholds.
templates.avoidWhen: Three peers (use Three Pillars) or before/after (use Before / After). Tables are dense by design — earn the density.

templates.name: Bar Chart Insight
templates.whenToUse: Bounded categorical comparisons where ordering matters — top-N institutions, error categories, monthly cohorts.
templates.avoidWhen: Continuous trends (use Timeline). Pipeline contract: SVG only — Chart.js is blocked by build-QA.

templates.name: Before / After
templates.whenToUse: Two-panel transformation — old way vs new way, raw data vs enriched, manual review vs automated. The narrative arc lives in the contrast.
templates.avoidWhen: Three-panel comparisons (use Three Pillars). The 50/50 split is what makes the rhetoric work.

templates.name: Old Way vs New Way
templates.whenToUse: Three to five matched rows showing transformation — process, latency, cost, accuracy. The arrow does the rhetorical work.
templates.avoidWhen: More than five rows (collapse to fewer pairs) or qualitative-only rows that don't earn their place (cut them).

templates.name: Step Flow
templates.whenToUse: Three sequential cards — process, evolution, lifecycle. Numbered, left-to-right, narrative carries the eye.
templates.avoidWhen: Loose categories that aren't actually a process (use Three Pillars). Sequencing without causality reads as filler.

templates.name: Flow Diagram
templates.whenToUse: A 4–6 node pipeline with arrows and one highlighted node. Better than Step Flow when the path branches or one node deserves emphasis.
templates.avoidWhen: Strict three-step process (use Step Flow). Long chains (>6 nodes) — break them into two slides.

templates.name: Architecture Map
templates.whenToUse: One central block (the platform) with many connected dependencies below. The 'big picture' slide.
templates.avoidWhen: Linear process (use Step Flow or Flow Diagram). Architecture means hierarchy + multiplicity, not just sequence.

templates.name: Timeline
templates.whenToUse: A 4–6 point horizontal timeline — product history, integration milestones, rollout phases. Time is the spine.
templates.avoidWhen: Items that aren't on a true time axis (use Step Flow). 'Future plans' slides — use Roadmap, it's more honest.

templates.name: Roadmap
templates.whenToUse: Four-column NOW / NEXT / LATER / VISION. The honest planning slide — communicates commitment levels without overpromising.
templates.avoidWhen: If the columns are equally weighted, you're not really planning. Earlier columns should be denser; later columns shorter.

templates.name: Proof Quote
templates.whenToUse: Customer testimonial paired with a hero stat — the 'why this is real' moment in a pitch deck.
templates.avoidWhen: Quotes without a named source. The attribution is the credibility. Vague 'a customer said' kills it.

templates.name: Code Window
templates.whenToUse: Show one API call or one component snippet — the developer experience proof point. Split layout: headline + body on the left, monospace pre on the right (10–15 lines max).
templates.avoidWhen: Stacked code under the title (use left/right split). Whole files (post a gist instead). Runtime libraries (highlight.js is blocked by build-QA — use pre with monospace).

templates.name: Action Cards
templates.whenToUse: The closer. Three audience-segmented action cards — engineering, product, executive. Or three rollout phases. The deck ends on a doable next step.
templates.avoidWhen: A single generic 'thanks' slide — that wastes the closing real estate. Always end with what to do next.
